planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8 weeks before last frost on the soil surface without covering (light improves germination); transplant to moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial shade. Great blue lobelia thrives at pond margins and rain garden edges.

directions: Start seeds on the surface of moist seed-starting mix under light 8 weeks before last frost; transplant to moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade at pond margins, stream banks, or rain garden edges where soil stays consistently wet. Great blue lobelia produces striking sky-blue flower spikes from late summer through fall that are magnets for hummingbirds and native bumblebees — the only pollinators strong enough to open the flower. It tolerates more shade and more moisture than most flowering perennials. Self-sows readily in moist sites to maintain persistent colonies. The closely related L. cardinalis (cardinal flower) has identical requirements but red flowers; growing them together creates a spectacular two-season hummingbird garden.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, average to moist soil in full sun; seeds benefit from cold stratification over winter for spring germination. Prairie blazing star grows from a corm and is also planted by corms in spring.

directions: Direct sow in fall in full sun in well-drained, average to slightly moist soil, or plant corms 2–3 inches deep in spring; prairie blazing star is a taller relative of the widely grown L. spicata, reaching 3–5 feet and producing dense, bottle-brush-like spikes of rose-purple flowers from top to bottom in midsummer — uniquely, the flowers open from the top of the spike downward. The plant grows from a corm (sometimes called a corn) and is extremely drought-tolerant once established in its native tallgrass prairie habitat. An exceptional nectar plant for migrating monarch butterflies, native bees, and hummingbirds. The dried seed heads are eaten by goldfinches and other songbirds. Excellent cut flower both fresh and dried.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist to average, well-drained soil in full sun; tall coreopsis self-sows freely and naturalizes in meadow plantings, prairie gardens, and along woodland edges.

directions: Direct sow in fall or early spring in moist to average, well-drained soil in full sun where the plant's height can be accommodated; tall coreopsis is the largest coreopsis species native to North America, reaching 4–8 feet and producing hundreds of cheerful, small yellow daisy-like flowers from midsummer through early fall. Unlike short-lived tickseed cultivars, the straight species is a robust, long-lived prairie perennial that establishes deeply and persists for decades without division. The anise-scented foliage is deer-resistant. Excellent for the back of meadow borders, along fences, and in rain garden margins where it adds bold vertical interest while feeding native bees and butterflies throughout the blooming season. Leave seed heads standing through winter for songbirds.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, fertile, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; cutleaf coneflower is one of the few tall yellow-flowered natives that performs well in partial shade and moist woodland edges.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, fertile, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun, giving plants 2–3 feet of space; cutleaf coneflower is a towering native perennial reaching 5–9 feet with deeply lobed leaves and bright yellow drooping-petaled flowers from midsummer through fall. Unlike most Rudbeckia species which demand full sun, this species tolerates partial shade and moist conditions, making it useful at the edges of woodland gardens and along shaded stream banks. The plant spreads vigorously by underground rhizomes and self-sows freely — give it room and expect it to colonize. Its large size provides structure and vertical interest in naturalistic plantings, and the seed heads feed goldfinches and other birds in fall and winter. Divide every 3–4 years in early spring to control spread.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; meadow phlox is more mildew-resistant than Phlox paniculata and better suited to humid climates.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade, spacing 18–24 inches apart with good air circulation; meadow phlox closely resembles garden phlox (Phlox paniculata) but produces cylindrical rather than domed flower clusters, blooms 2–3 weeks earlier in early summer, and is significantly more resistant to powdery mildew — an important advantage in hot, humid climates. Flower color ranges from white to pink-lavender with a darker eye; fragrance is excellent. Shear plants by half after the first bloom flush to encourage a second wave of flowers in late summer. The cultivar 'Natasha' with bicolor pink-and-white flowers is particularly striking and disease-resistant. Divide every 3 years.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, poor to average, slightly alkaline soil in full sun; prairie coneflower thrives on neglect in dry, lean soils and self-sows freely to maintain naturalized meadow colonies.

directions: Direct sow in fall in poor, dry, well-drained, slightly alkaline soil in full sun, barely pressing seeds into the surface; prairie coneflower (also called Mexican hat) is one of the most drought-tolerant native prairie wildflowers, producing distinctive flowers with a tall, thimble-shaped central column from which yellow or red-and-yellow ray petals droop like a Mexican sombrero, blooming from early summer through fall. Self-sows abundantly in dry, lean conditions to form spreading meadow drifts. The foliage has a pleasant anise-like fragrance and the plant tolerates heavy clay, rocky soil, and summer drought with equal indifference. An excellent addition to xeriscape, roadside restoration, and butterfly garden plantings across the Great Plains, Southwest, and Gulf Coast.

planting.note: Direct sow scarified seed in fall in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; baptisia develops a deep taproot and dislikes transplanting — choose the permanent site carefully as plants are long-lived in place.

directions: Direct sow scarified seed in fall in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic soil in full sun; white wild indigo takes 3–5 years to reach full size from seed but is essentially permanent once established, producing elegant, multi-branched blue-green shrub-like mounds with white pea-family flowers in mid-spring and inflated, rattle-like seed pods that persist through winter providing garden interest and bird food. It fixes nitrogen, is deer-resistant, drought-tolerant, and thrives in clay soils — a remarkably tough, long-lived perennial that improves with age. The blue-green foliage is attractive throughout the growing season. Do not transplant established plants; the deep taproot makes this nearly impossible. Choose placement carefully at installation.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
